event: Moses Strikes the Rock at Horeb
symbols: staff striking the rock — divine power channeled through the mediator to sustain the people
water flowing from rock — God’s miraculous provision in the barren wilderness
people gathering water in jars — the community’s desperate need met by divine grace
desert mountains — the harsh testing ground where faith is refined
description: A scene set in a rocky desert landscape with mountains in the background. At the upper right, a robed figure with a raised staff stands on elevated rocks, from which water gushes forth. Below, men and women in Middle Eastern robes and head coverings gather eagerly around the water stream, filling jars and pots. Some kneel to drink while others carry vessels. The figures express urgency and relief. The setting is arid and sun-baked, emphasizing the miracle of water in the wilderness.
theological_description: Moses striking the rock at Horeb (Exodus 17:1-7) is one of the defining miracles of the wilderness period. When the Israelites complained bitterly about having no water, God commanded Moses to strike the rock with his staff, and water gushed forth abundantly. Paul identifies this rock typologically with Christ: ‘They drank from the spiritual Rock that followed them, and the Rock was Christ’ (1 Corinthians 10:4). Jesus himself drew on this imagery at the Feast of Tabernacles: ‘If anyone thirsts, let him come to me and drink’ (John 7:37). The place was named Massah and Meribah (‘testing’ and ‘quarreling’), marking a crucial episode in Israel’s struggle between faith and doubt. The miracle demonstrates that God provides for his people even in their rebellion, sustaining them not because they deserve it but because of his covenant faithfulness. Moses’ later striking of the rock in anger (Numbers 20:11) cost him entry to the Promised Land, showing that even mediators must honor God’s holiness.

scripture_refs.reason: Moses striking the rock at Horeb and water flowing forth to quench the people’s thirst
The spiritual Rock that followed them was Christ
Jesus declaring himself the source of living water at the Feast of Tabernacles
The second striking of the rock and Moses’ failure to honor God’s holiness
He split rocks in the wilderness and gave them drink abundantly
